summary: Partially decompiled the sword item by adding a large C implementation for `src/melee/it/items/itsword.c`, moving most text out of `asm/melee/it/items/itsword.s`, wiring the new object into `obj_files.mk`, and adding item-specific headers/types for sword article data and per-item variables. The match report showed the new file at 98.27% overall, with many functions at 100%; remaining weaker spots included `it_802852B8` and `itSword_Spawn`. No review comments were present in the slice.
what_changed: Added a new 1927-line C source file for the sword item, `src/melee/it/items/itsword.c`, with literals, an `ItemStateTable it_803F5800`, many static callback prototypes, and C versions of most functions previously in `asm/melee/it/items/itsword.s`.
Removed about 1432 lines of hand-assembly text/data from `asm/melee/it/items/itsword.s`, leaving the PR as a substantial asm-to-C migration while still keeping the asm object listed.
Added `$(BUILD_DIR)/src/melee/it/items/itsword.c.o` to `obj_files.mk` immediately before the residual `itsword.s.o`.
Renamed the known spawn entrypoint from the raw symbol `it_80284D54` to `itSword_Spawn` and updated a Bob-omb rain call site in `it_26B1.c` to call `itSword_Spawn(&bobOmbRain->x8_vec)`.
Renamed the shield callback from `it_80286048` to `itSword_BounceOffShield` and updated the sword entry in `it_803F14C4` in `it_3F14.c`.
Changed `Item_80268B5C` from a `void` API to returning `HSD_GObj*`/`Item_GObj*`, matching its use by `itSword_Spawn`, where the spawned item is conditionally initialized via `it_80285424`.
Added `itSword_ItemVars` to `itCommonItems.h` and inserted it into the `Item` union as `ip->xDD4_itemVar.sword`, documenting offsets from `ip+DD4` through `ip+E2C`.
Added item-local forward and types headers, including `itSword_UnkArticle1` and `itSword_UnkBytes`, used for sword article special attributes.
Reformatted or clarified `SpawnItem` field layout in `it/types.h` and copied a Doxygen-documented version into `types.dox`.
Adjusted includes in `ftcommon.c`, `it_26B1.c`, and `itsword.h` so callers use the new sword-specific header rather than broad declarations in `it_27CF.h`.
Changed `tools/convert_data.py`'s `input_glob` from `**/ft/ftcoll.s` to `**/it/items/itsword.s`, apparently to target data conversion work for this item.
smart_moves: Kept known-but-not-fully-matching functions under `ASM` wrappers with a C implementation guarded by `#if !defined(MUST_MATCH) || defined(WIP)` and an inline-assembly fallback for stricter matching builds; this preserves progress while documenting intended C logic.
Promoted behaviorally understood symbols to semantic names only where evidence was strong: `itSword_Spawn` and `itSword_BounceOffShield`, while leaving most unknown functions as address-based names.
Fixed the return type of the shared spawn helper `Item_80268B5C` instead of working around it locally, making the sword spawn implementation type-correct and reusable by future item decompilation.

Introduced explicit item-variable and article-attribute structs before translating much of the logic, giving the decompiled code readable access through `ip->xDD4_itemVar.sword` instead of raw offsets.
Preserved address comments in the header prototypes, making it easy to cross-reference the decompiled functions with original addresses and match reports.
Documented duplicate-code concerns directly in `itsword.c` with a TODO about possible future inline cleanup, without risking match regressions during this partial-match PR.
decomp_lessons: For item files with many repeated state/tween helper patterns, first model the per-item variable block in `xDD4_itemVar`; repeated offset clusters can become a named item-vars struct even when fields are still `xNN`.

When a common helper is discovered to return a value, update the shared declaration and implementation rather than casting or ignoring the mismatch at call sites; this can unlock clean spawn decompilation.

It is acceptable in this project to partially migrate an item from asm to C with `ASM` functions and guarded inline assembly for stubborn functions, as long as the match report and object wiring remain clear.

Use semantic names sparingly and evidence-groundedly: spawn functions and item collision callbacks are good candidates when call sites and logic clearly show behavior; less-understood state callbacks can remain address named.

Item headers benefit from address-prefixed prototypes, item-specific forward declarations, and small item-local `types.h` files to avoid bloating global headers with incomplete special-attribute structs.
When replacing declarations previously housed in broad headers like `it_27CF.h`, move them to the specific item header and update callers to include that item header directly.
naming_conventions: Sword-specific semantic functions use the `itSword_` prefix, as in `itSword_Spawn` and `itSword_BounceOffShield`.
Unknown or not-yet-understood sword functions retain address-based names such as `it_80284E10`, `it_802852B8`, and `it_80285B00`.
Item-variable structs follow item-specific names with raw field offsets: `struct itSword_ItemVars` fields `x0`, `x4`, `x8`, etc., with comments tying them to `ip+DD4`, `ip+DD8`, and later offsets.
Item-local unknown article structs were named `itSword_UnkArticle1` and `itSword_UnkBytes`, reflecting limited semantic confidence while still scoping them to sword.
Header prototypes include original address comments, e.g. `/* 284D54 */ Item_GObj* itSword_Spawn(Vec3* pos);`.
The `Item` union member for sword variables is lower-case `sword`, accessed as `ip->xDD4_itemVar.sword`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: The PR used `ASM` function declarations plus guarded C/inline-assembly bodies for non-perfect matches, notably `itSword_Spawn` and several update helpers.

The match report recorded `src/melee/it/items/itsword.c` at 98.27% overall, with many functions at 100%, but `it_802852B8` at 33.33% and `itSword_Spawn` at 85.43%, supporting the 'partial match' title.
Constants formerly emitted from assembly were represented as `LITERAL` C constants such as `it_804DC8D0 = 0`, `it_804DC8D4 = -1`, `it_804DC8D8 = 0.3`, and `it_804DC8E8 = S32_TO_F32`.
Repeated frame/scale interpolation logic was expressed through the new `ip->xDD4_itemVar.sword` fields rather than raw offsets, which likely helped produce many exact matches.

The C implementation uses existing engine helpers such as `Item_80268B5C`, `HSD_JObjSetScaleY`, `HSD_JObjSetScale`, `itColl_BounceOffShield`, and `it_8026B894` to mirror the original assembly behavior.
`tools/convert_data.py` was retargeted to `**/it/items/itsword.s`, suggesting data/literal conversion support was part of the workflow for this decomp slice.
follow_up_queries: Look for later PRs that complete matching `src/melee/it/items/itsword.c`, especially `it_802852B8`, `itSword_Spawn`, `it_80285B00`, `it_80285804`, and `it_80285920`.
Search whether the committed `tools/convert_data.py` `input_glob` retargeting to `itsword.s` was later reverted or generalized.
Find later renames of `itSword_ItemVars` fields or `itSword_UnkArticle1` fields to recover semantics for the sword animation/scale variables.
Search for other item PRs that reused the `Item_80268B5C` return-type correction or the `ASM` guarded-C matching pattern.
Check whether the TODO about duplicate code in `itsword.c` was resolved with inlines or shared helpers in later decomp work.
